workspace-onboarding._note_2026_05_07: Three bugs fixed during G4b6 cold-launch verification first run. (1) `POST /api/workspace/init` is now idempotent: `services/workspace_service.create_workspace()` returns `{status: 'exists', workspace_path}` when called against an already-initialized workspace instead of raising `WorkspaceExistsError` (which was surfaced as HTTP 409 / 'Conflict'). The wizard's `Open Jarvis` button always re-calls this endpoint after the first-run orchestrator has already created the workspace at sidecar startup, so the 409 fired on every cold-launch — error text was 'Conflict' (the HTTP reason phrase), not the body's `detail`, so onboarding.vue's `msg.includes('already' || 'exists')` swallow-logic didn't catch it. Removed the `WorkspaceExistsError` class entirely (no longer used), removed the `except WorkspaceExistsError` branch from `routers/workspace.py`, and dropped `status_code=201` from the route decorator (single 200 response keeps the idempotent shape simple). `pages/onboarding.vue` swallow-by-substring removed — any error from initWorkspace now propagates honestly. Tests updated: `test_create_workspace_twice_raises` → `test_create_workspace_twice_is_idempotent`, `test_post_init_duplicate` → `test_post_init_duplicate_is_idempotent`, `test_post_init_creates_workspace` 201 → 200. Full backend suite: 23 workspace + 29 first-run-orchestrator tests pass, no regressions. (2) Wizard `pulling_primary` progress text rendered Ollama's raw `status` field — strings like `pulling a3de86cd1c13` (the layer hash) leaked into the wizard UI as visible copy. Added `humanizePullStatus()` helper in `OnboardingLocalFlow.vue` mapping known Ollama status patterns to user copy: `pulling manifest` → 'Preparing', `pulling <hash>` → 'Downloading', `verifying` → 'Verifying', `writing manifest` → 'Finalizing', `removing` → 'Cleaning up', `success` → 'Done'. Unknown future statuses fall through with first-letter capitalization rather than being swallowed — better to surface a new code than mask it. The progress bar + bytes counter already carries the real signal; this just supplies a clean phase label. (3) StatusBar 'Checking…' pill stayed stuck on the onboarding page because `useAppState().checkHealth()` was only invoked in `pages/main.vue:onMounted` — the wizard never mounts /main, so `backendStatus` stayed at 'unknown' for the entire onboarding flow even though the sidecar was healthy. Moved the `checkHealth()` call into `layouts/default.vue:onMounted` (fires on every page that uses the default layout — i.e. every page) and added a 30 s repeat interval (cleared on unmount) so a backend that goes down mid-session doesn't leave the pill stuck on stale 'Alive'. The redundant `checkHealth()` call in `main.vue:onMounted` is left in place — harmless duplicate now that the layout fires first; removing it requires verifying nothing else in main.vue depends on the call sequence. vue-tsc clean (rc=0).

chat._note_2026_05_08: ML warmup service landed end-to-end — closes the 'turn 2 of cold app start takes 25-30 s' bug that remained after the per-WS outbound send queue (`_note_2026_05_07b`) and the spaCy thread-offload patch in `session_service._save_session_bg`. Diagnosis: turn 1 of a cold session has empty history, so the retrieval pipeline short-circuits and never touches the fastembed embedder + reranker; turn 1's `_save_session_bg` is the *first* call site that touches spaCy (the `xx_ent_wiki_sm` model lazy-loads ~2 s on the event loop, even after the to-thread wrapper). Turn 2 then both runs the retrieval pipeline (lazy-loads embedder ~3 s + reranker ~5-8 s) AND contends with the still-loading spaCy import — producing the observed 25-30 s wall-clock stall. The fix moves all of those costs to sidecar boot. New `backend/services/warmup_service.py` exercises each component once on dummy input in a background daemon thread (`threading.Thread`, never on the event loop): tokenizer (primes the `tokenizers` Rust extension by walking `_BUNDLED_TOKENIZER_IDS`), spaCy NER (`extract_entities` on synthetic text), fastembed embedder (`embed_query('warmup')` — exercises both model load + query-prefix path), fastembed reranker (`rerank('warmup query', [doc1, doc2])`). Sequential ordering (tokenizer → NER → embedder → reranker) chosen so that if a chat dispatch races warmup, the smallest-and-most-likely-needed components finish first; parallel ONNX loads contend for the same CPU + memory bandwidth so they're not actually faster. Status state is thread-safe (Lock-guarded dict), each component progresses pending → running → ready/failed/skipped (`skipped` for `JARVIS_DISABLE_RERANKER=1` or stripped tokenizers package, NOT an error). `start()` is idempotent — second call is a no-op; per-component failures don't abort the loop (orchestrator wraps each warmer in try/except so a rogue future warmer can't take down the whole sequence). New `GET /api/health/warm` endpoint surfaces `{ready, started, completed, started_at, completed_at, components: {name: {state, duration_ms, error}}}` — always 200 (readiness is a payload field, not an HTTP status, so the route is cheap to hit during loading). Wired into the FastAPI lifespan after `start_workers()` — non-blocking, the `yield` proceeds immediately. Frontend gets `useWarmup()` composable (module-level singleton — only one poll loop across the app), polled with backoff schedule `[250, 250, 500, 500, 1000, 1000, 2000]` ms then 2 s steady-state until `ready=true`, started once at the layout level (`default.vue::onMounted`). `ChatPanel.vue` renders an instrument-readout pill above the messages area (`PREPARING MODELS · tokenizer · NER · embedder · reranker`) when `started && !ready`; phosphor-amber pulse dot matches `ChatFirstTurnWarmup`'s aesthetic; per-component states styled (running=amber, ready/skipped=line-through, failed=neon-orange); transitions in/out via Vue `<Transition>` so it disappears the instant warmup completes. Tests: 10 unit tests in `test_warmup_service.py` covering initial pending state, idempotency of `start()`, all-components-reach-terminal-state on stubs, per-component failure isolation, `wait_for_ready` timeout + completion contracts, `started_at`/`completed_at` wallclock invariants, and the `/api/health/warm` schema shape (verifies the endpoint renders cleanly in tests where the lifespan doesn't run, since the snapshot must be readable from the pending state). Full backend suite: 1772 passed (was 1762; +10 from warmup tests). Frontend production build clean (vue-tsc + nuxi build). The earlier `_note_2026_05_07c` (catalog visibility) entry stays — that change is independent and still correct. UX consequence: the existing `ChatFirstTurnWarmup` 4-stage surface keeps its purpose (Ollama model load + first prefill on the actual user turn) but its preconditions are now better — by the time the user dispatches their first message, the ML caches are already warm, so the wait collapses to just Ollama's mmap + prefill (~10-15 s on M5 / qwen3:8b / 2.7K-token prompt). Turn 2 now runs through pre-warmed components, eliminating the 25-30 s stall observed across builds #9-13.
chat._note_2026_05_07b: Per-WS outbound send queue landed — fixes the real cause of the 'second message takes 24 s' bug that ADR 016 tried but failed to resolve. Diagnosis from build #9 sidecar.log on M5 24 GB: `chat_step received js_block_ms=0.0 wire_time_ms=24466.3 transport=http` on turn 2, but the sidecar was completely silent for 28 s between turn 1's `chat_turn` log line (13:52:09) and turn 2's `chat_step received` (13:52:38) — no httpx access log, no uvicorn route log, nothing. That silence is the smoking gun: ADR 016's `wire_time_ms` diagnostic stamps `_now_ms` *inside* the per-session dispatch lock (acquired at `_dispatch()` line 1212), so `wire_time_ms` includes lock-wait time, not just transport latency. The HTTP request actually arrived promptly; it just couldn't acquire the lock because turn 1 was still holding it. Turn 1 was holding the lock because `await _send_event(ws, 'done', ...)` at chat.py:1056 was blocked on `ws.send_json` — WKWebView App Nap had paused the JS-side WS read pump after the user's UI rendered the final response and went idle, the kernel TCP receive buffer filled, server's send blocked. When the user typed turn 2 and pressed Enter, WKWebView woke, drained the buffered 'done' frame, the server's await unblocked, the lock released, and turn 2's already-queued dispatch finally ran. Architectural fix: new `backend/services/ws_send_queue.py::WSSendQueue` decouples backend dispatch from WS write back-pressure. Each WS gets a bounded `asyncio.Queue` (default maxsize=256) plus a single consumer task that drains FIFO and writes to the WS. `routers/chat.py::_send_event` becomes a fast `await queue.put(payload)` that returns once the frame is enqueued, not when the kernel has flushed. The dispatch lock is released as soon as model work completes — the trailing 'done' send happens in the consumer task on its own. Frame ordering preserved by single-consumer-per-WS (TCP serializes writes hitting the same socket). Bounded queue means a permanently-stuck consumer can't leak memory; `enqueue()` blocks with a 2 s timeout when full and returns False on overflow. Send failures fail-latch: once `ws.send_json` raises, the consumer exits and subsequent enqueues fast-return False, leaving the WS receive loop to handle WebSocketDisconnect. WS handler attaches the queue at `await websocket.accept()` and closes it (with 1 s drain timeout) in the `finally` block on disconnect. Tests: 12 unit tests in `test_ws_send_queue.py` covering FIFO + producer-consumer decoupling + fail-latching + close semantics + idempotency + post-close reject; 4 integration tests added to `test_chat_ws.py` — `test_send_event_routes_through_queue_when_attached` (events go through enqueue path), `test_send_event_does_not_block_on_stuck_ws_when_queue_attached` (the load-bearing property: a wedged ws.send_json does not couple subsequent _send_event calls; 5 follow-up sends complete in <0.5 s while first send is held), `test_send_event_falls_back_to_direct_send_without_queue` (backwards-compat for test/dev paths), `test_ws_handler_attaches_and_detaches_send_queue` (E2E via TestClient with attach/detach spies). Full backend suite: 1762 passed (was 1746; +16 from the new tests). The earlier `_note_2026_05_07` entry (microstate retune) stays — that change is still correct given the post-prefix-stable baseline; the perception fix and the lock-coupling fix are separate concerns. ADR 016's diagnostic log line keeps the `wire_time_ms` field for future regression-detection but the field name is now misleading — leaving it for now; renaming requires updating the wire-time assertion logic in `chat.py` and keeping the diagnostic continuous across builds.

chat._note_2026_05_04b: ADR 016 landed end-to-end: chat sends now route through Tauri Rust IPC (`send_chat_message` command) → loopback HTTP `POST /api/chat/message` → existing `_handle_message`. Streaming response still flows back over the WebSocket; only the SEND direction is split off. Root cause that forced this: build #11 wire-time diagnostic (`js_block_ms` + `wire_time_ms` injected into every payload) proved that the second user message of every fresh app session sat for 27 s in the WKWebView's outbound WebSocket queue before reaching the backend. JS-side blocking was 0 ms, transport was 27 s. macOS WKWebView throttles in-page outbound WebSocket I/O after the view is idle (App Nap / power-management), and the throttle does not affect WebSocket receive (which is why streaming back was always clean) and does not affect network I/O initiated from the Rust shell (which has its own URLSession outside the WebView's). Backend gains: `_active_sessions: dict[str, {ws, get_llm, lock}]` registry; `_validate_message_dict` extracted from `_parse_message`; `_process_chat_payload` extracted from the WS receive loop and reused by both transports; `POST /api/chat/message` endpoint that looks up the session WS, validates, and dispatches as a background task (returns 200 immediately, errors surface as WS `error` events). Per-session `asyncio.Lock` serialises concurrent dispatches whether they arrive WS or HTTP. Tauri shell gains: `reqwest` dep (default-features off, just `json` + `http2` — no TLS for loopback), `BackendUrlHandle` + `HttpClient` state, `#[tauri::command] async fn send_chat_message(payload, backend, client)` registered via `invoke_handler`. Frontend gains: `_inTauri()` env-detector via `'__TAURI_INTERNALS__' in window`; `useWebSocket.send()` takes the IPC path for `{type:'message'}` frames in Tauri, falls back to `ws.send()` in browser dev. Heartbeat pings stay on the WS — they need to exercise THIS socket so the staleness detector keeps a real signal. The wire-time log line gains a `transport=http|ws` field so we can observe the fix in production logs and watch for regressions. `@tauri-apps/api` added to package deps. ADR 016 records the full architectural reasoning + rejected alternatives. Backend chat suite (chat_ws/chat_compaction/chat_metrics) green at 25 passed.
chat._note_2026_05_04: WebSocket transport now detects stale TCP sockets. Symptom diagnosed on M5 24 GB after a multi-hour idle gap: backend was completely silent for 23 s between user-bubble timestamp and `chat_step after_add_message`, even though `readyState === OPEN` and `ws.send()` returned synchronously. Root cause: macOS App Nap / OS sleep / NAT timeout silently kills the underlying TCP connection without transitioning the JS WebSocket out of OPEN; ws.send() succeeded into the OS buffer and the data sat in TCP retransmits for ~23 s before either rehydrating the path or finally arriving. The frontend's heartbeat was one-way (ping every 25 s, nothing listened for a reply) so there was no inbound liveness signal during idle. Fix is two-sided. Backend: `chat.py` now responds to `{type:'ping'}` with `{type:'pong'}` instead of silently dropping it (5-line change in the WS receive loop). Frontend: `useWebSocket.ts` tracks `_lastPongAt` (refreshed on every inbound frame, including pongs which are NOT broadcast to chat listeners), and `send()` checks `Date.now() - _lastPongAt > 30 s` before trusting `readyState === OPEN`. On a stale verdict it pushes the frame to a small outbound queue (cap 8), force-closes the socket with `_suppressNextDisconnect=true` so the close handler doesn't broadcast a 'disconnected' event to chat consumers (which would flash an error and reset isLoading), and immediately reconnects. The new `onopen` drains the queue so the message that triggered the reconnect rides the fresh connection — user just sees a brief delay (~200–500 ms reconnect), not a 23 s stall. Force-reconnect is suppression-based but single-use: if the new connection also fails, that close fires `disconnected` normally and the user sees a real error. Also added: guard against double-scheduling reconnect timers, guard against starting a second connect when one is already in CONNECTING state. New event type `WsPong` added to types/index.ts WsEvent union for completeness — listeners never receive it because the inbound handler short-circuits on `data.type === 'pong'`. No unit test added — the existing `useWebSocket.test.ts` only exercises the MockWebSocket harness rather than the composable, and the new behavior is awkward to mock without auto-import scaffolding; manual M5 test pending after rebuild.
chat._note_2026_05_01g: First-turn bootstrap surface (`ChatFirstTurnWarmup.vue`) replaces the generic typing-dots indicator on turn 1 of a fresh session. Even after the prefix-stable architectural fix landed (note `_note_2026_05_01f`), turn 1 still pays a one-time bootstrap cost on M5 24 GB — ~16s for cold model load + first re-prefill of the ~2.7K-token prompt prefix. Three generic dots over a 16s wait reads as 'broken'; users assume that's the steady-state speed and never see turn 2's <2s. The new component communicates honestly: instrument-panel aesthetic (monospace-only, hairline borders, phosphor-amber accent on active stage), four-stage progress (load model / read notes / compose first / stream) animated by elapsed time, real-time elapsed counter alongside a calibrated 15s estimate, and one line of microcopy stating 'Follow-up replies typically land in 1-2s.' Stage transitions are time-driven heuristic rather than telemetry-driven (Ollama doesn't emit per-stage signals during a turn) — the timings are calibrated against measured M5 + qwen3:8b + 2.7K-prefix numbers so the user's mental model tracks reality. Strictly one-shot: gated on `messages.some(m => m.role === 'assistant')` being false; once an assistant turn has rendered, the surface never appears again for that session. Reduced-motion users get a static surface; the timer continues to update. Light-mode adapts cleanly via `@media (prefers-color-scheme: light)`. Only consumer-side rendering change; no backend signal needed. Type-checks clean (vue-tsc exit 0). Live verification on the bundled .app is the next step: rebuild and confirm the surface appears on turn 1 of a fresh session and disappears the instant the first token streams.
chat._note_2026_05_01f: ADR 009 amendment 2026-05-01 landed — stable system-prompt prefix (retrieval moves to user-message position). The bug confirmed in note `_note_2026_05_01e` is now fixed structurally. `build_system_prompt_with_stats` no longer embeds retrieved-context into the system prompt; it returns the retrieval block as `stats['retrieval_block']` instead, and the chat router glues it onto the latest user message before dispatch via the new `attach_retrieval_to_user_message` helper. The system prompt becomes byte-stable across turns within a session (persona + specialist directives + JARVIS extensions + language reminder), so Ollama's KV cache prefix-match reuses the long stable prefix on warm follow-up turns — only the just-asked user turn (small) gets prefilled. Empirically this collapses warm TTFT from ~7.7 s to under 1 s on M5 24 GB at the same hardware-bound decode tok/s. No quality regression: same retrieval pipeline, same notes retrieved, same XML wrapping, same trace surfaced to the frontend. Compaction interacts cleanly because retrieval is attached AFTER compaction runs (so old turns in history are clean, no stale retrieval leaks into recall blocks) and the `_maybe_compact` headroom math now subtracts `system_prompt_tokens + retrieval_tokens` rather than just `system_prompt_tokens`. The `_prefill_log` diagnostic gains an `rb_hash` field so a future regression where retrieval leaks back into the system prompt would still show as `prefix_stable=False`. Language-reminder text updated to be position-agnostic ('any retrieved notes attached to the user's message' instead of 'the notes above'). New test file `backend/tests/test_system_prompt_stable_prefix.py` (11 tests) pins the contract; full backend suite at 1,434 passed / 1 skipped after the change. Eval runner updated to mirror the new shape via `attach_retrieval_to_user_message`. ADR 009 §Amendment 2026-05-01 records the architectural shift end-to-end. Two open verification gates remain: (a) full conversation eval re-baseline against the existing canonical baseline (`baselines/run-20260428T112547Z.json`) — manual run on workstation; (b) bundled-app rebuild + M5 chat_turn diagnostic verification that `prefix_stable=True` and `prefill_ms < 1000` on turn 2+.
chat._note_2026_05_01e: Diagnostic confirmed the hypothesis on M5 24 GB. Two real-world turns captured (turn 1 cold / turn 2 warm): sp_hash mutated turn-to-turn (640883b89a4b → a5f528fc18e1), prefix_stable=False on both, prefill_count ~2,750 each (full re-prefill), ttft_ms 14,682 cold and 7,744 warm. So the system-prompt prefix-instability bug is real and is responsible for the warm-turn TTFT floor. ALSO surfaced a second, separate problem the diagnostic exposed: decode_tps caps at ~22 tok/s on M5 with Ollama 0.18.0 + embedded GGML-Metal-fallback kernels (the version we're pinned to per memory `bundled-ollama-pinned-0.18.0-m5` because 0.21.2+ SIGABRT). Theoretical M5 ceiling for an 8B Q4 model is ~100 tok/s — kernels are under-utilizing the hardware by 4-5×. Architectural fix (move retrieval out of system prompt for KV cache reuse) addresses prefill but not decode. Decode side requires a runtime-shaped decision: Apple MLX migration, speculative decoding, MoE default for Apple Silicon, or upstream Ollama Metal 4 fix landing. New investigation folder `docs/research/local-inference-runtime/` files this as 6 open topic briefs (README + 01-MLX-benchmarks + 02-speculative-decoding + 03-Ollama-M5-regression + 04-MoE-on-Apple-Silicon + 05-tool-format-efficiency + 06-competitive-runtime-scan) — the diagnostic numbers in this note are the baseline measurements those briefs anchor against.
chat._note_2026_05_01d: Added a temporary per-turn prefill-cost diagnostic in `chat.py::_prefill_log`. Investigation of 'every-turn 30s+ TTFT' reported during G4b6 cold-launch smoke. Hypothesis under test: the system prompt embeds retrieval output, so the prefix changes turn-to-turn → Ollama's KV cache prefix-match fails at token 0 → full re-prefill (~3-5K tokens) every turn. The logger emits one structured INFO line per chat turn (`chat_turn session=… turn=N sp_hash=… prefix_stable=True/False sp_total_tok=… prefill_count=… prefill_ms=… ttft_ms=… load_ms=… decode_tps=… …`) so we can confirm or reject the hypothesis from the sidecar log without scraping the WS payload. Per-session state (turn counter + last system-prompt SHA12) capped at 256 sessions FIFO so it can't grow unbounded. Hooked into `_handle_message` after `_build_metrics_payload`, wrapped in try/except so a diagnostic glitch can't break the turn. To remove cleanly: delete the `_PERF_TURN_STATE`/`_prefill_log` block and its single callsite. Tests still green (test_chat_memory_pressure + test_ollama_dispatcher).
chat._note_2026_05_01c: Per-turn telemetry surface + chat-health watcher landed end-to-end (ADR 005 §C trigger 2 amendment). Backend: `StreamEvent` gains `eval_duration_ns / prompt_eval_duration_ns / load_duration_ns / total_duration_ns` (all optional); `OllamaDispatcher` populates them from Ollama's `done` chunk; `chat.py` accumulates per-turn metrics across rounds (decode + prefill durations sum, TTFT taken from first round's load + prefill — the felt initial latency) into a `metrics_acc` list threaded through `_handle_message` and `_stream_follow_up`, rendered into a `metrics` payload on the WS `done` event when any round reported timings. Frontend: new `ChatTurnMetrics` type on `ChatMessage`; `useChat` attaches it to assistant messages and forwards `decode_tps` to `useChatHealth.recordTurn(model, decodeTps)`. New composable `useChatHealth.ts` loads probe baselines from `GET /api/local/chat-model-probe` (per-machine `realistic_tps` indexed by `ollama_model`), rolls a 5-turn per-model window, classifies as `healthy`/`slow`/`fast`/`unknown`, and emits soft-hint snackbar toasts on sustained drift — slow at ratio < 0.5 (10 min cooldown), fast at ratio > 1.05 with a heavier installed-and-probed rung available (24 h cooldown). Both toasts deep-link to `/settings#local-models` for an in-place re-test. ChatPanel renders a compact mono `12.4 t/s · 0.85s` pill in the existing meta row, tinted by health status (amber slow / soft green fast / cyan healthy), with a `title` tooltip carrying the full breakdown; an inline 'Re-test models' advisory banner shows for the duration of any sustained-slow window. Strict non-goals: never gates dispatch, never auto-swaps. ADR 005 §C trigger 2 amended in place; chat.md gained a 'Per-turn telemetry & health watcher' subsection. CLAUDE.md amended to forbid splitting features into 'passive surface lands now / active behavior later' chunks — there's no release cadence to justify it.
chat._note_2026_05_01b: OllamaDispatcher.stream_response now always passes `think: False`. Without it, qwen3-family models emit a `<think>…</think>` block per turn that surfaces as `message.thinking` tokens with empty `message.content` for 5–10 s of perceived dead air before the first content delta. Direct `/api/chat` measurement on M5 + qwen3:8b warm: 0.89 s end-to-end with `think:false`, ~10 s without. Catalog models that don't support thinking (gemma4, ministral, devstral, gpt-oss) silently ignore the flag — no harm. Models that should honor it but leak (the ADR-012 'fail_correctness' class) are caught by the chat-model-probe pre-screen, so they don't get auto-recommended. Test added: `test_ollama_dispatcher.py` asserts `kwargs['think'] is False` on the chat call.
chat._note_2026_05_01: Disabled the auto-downgrade pre-flight memory-pressure swap (ADR 005 §C trigger 2) in `_apply_memory_pressure_swap`. The check gated dispatch on `psutil.virtual_memory().available × 0.8 ≥ effective_footprint`, but on macOS unified memory `psutil.available` excludes the reclaimable inactive/cached pool that Ollama mmap-loading uses — producing the very 'Insufficient RAM: no installed local model fits' failure mode it was meant to prevent. Empirical: 24 GB Apple Silicon refused an 8B model in-app while a 30B loaded fine from the terminal. The lightweight-mode hard-pin (trigger 3) and OOM-retry-walk-the-ladder (trigger 1) remain — actual safety net is the OOM retry, not the pre-flight. Test suite updated: deleted `test_preflight_floor_refusal_short_circuits_before_llm` (path is gone), all 8 remaining test_chat_memory_pressure tests pass.
chat._note_2026_04_29c: Second code-review pass on the ADR 009 wiring closed 2 medium + 2 low follow-ups introduced or surfaced by the first fix pass; full suite 1497 passed / 204 skipped after. (1) _save_to_memory_locks dict cleaned up in both _evict_oldest_sessions and delete_session — the per-session asyncio.Lock was leaking one entry per ever-seen session id for the lifetime of the process. (2) Tightened the consecutive-think-blocks test: the original was named 'no consecutive same-role messages' but only asserted on assistant pairs, allowing user-user pairs through silently. Split into two tests — one pinning that pure-think blocks are dropped (matches what the function actually does on unusual histories), and a new test_strip_preserves_alternation_on_normal_history pinning the real production contract that on alternating histories think-stripping never produces consecutive same-role messages of either kind. (3) _enforce_system_prompt_budget now returns the base/context/lang token counts it computed alongside (context, was_truncated), so build_system_prompt_with_stats reuses them instead of re-encoding the same strings — saves three HF tokenizer encodes per turn on the hot path; falls back to fresh counts when the helper short-circuits. (4) Clarified the comment in save_session_to_memory that the messages parameter is a live reference to session['messages'] rather than a defensive snapshot — concurrent add_message calls are visible inside the locked section, which is benign (saved note is fresher) but worth documenting.
chat._note_2026_04_29b: Code-review pass on the ADR 009 wiring chunk applied 2 critical, 3 high, 5 medium, 2 low fixes; full suite 1492 passed / 204 skipped after. Changes: (1) _synthesize_retrieval_block now emits role='assistant' rather than 'user' — the kept window's first message is always a real user turn so a user-role substitution block produced consecutive same-role messages, which Anthropic accepts but Ollama chat templates can merge or reject; assistant-role reads naturally as 'recalling earlier context' and alternates cleanly. Compaction is gated to provider=='ollama' so Anthropic's 'first must be user' constraint is moot. (2) _enforce_system_prompt_budget now budgets the truncation marker against context_budget and re-shrinks if the marker would push it over; the ADR 009 invariant 'assembled prompt fits the budget' now always holds (previously the post-loop append could overflow by ~5–15% on dense token content). (3) compact_messages logs a warning when post-compaction tokens_after > headroom (large vault snippets pushing the kept window back over budget) so an operator can page on the silent-overflow case. (4) _maybe_compact now wraps the entire compaction path (catalog lookup + token counting + compaction + recording + WS emit) in one try/except so any unhandled exception degrades to 'use uncompacted history' rather than dropping the WS connection. (5) record_compaction_event now triggers _auto_persist immediately so a process crash inside the LLM stream — or a non-swallowed tool-loop exception — can't lose an audit event between recording and the next assistant add_message; tests' _no_auto_persist autouse fixture neutralises this in test runs. (6) save_session_to_memory now serialises concurrent saves of the same session via a per-session asyncio.Lock; previously a debounced background save racing with a WebSocketDisconnect explicit save could produce two notes for one logical session, leaking past the find_earlier_turn_context same-session exclusion. (7) JARVIS_COMPACTION_RECENT_N is now clamped to [floor=2, ceiling=200] with a warning on out-of-range values — symmetric with the threshold_pct clamp. (8) build_system_prompt_with_stats now uses count_tokens with the tokenizer for stats too, not char/4, so the audit logs match what compaction and budget enforcement actually saw on Polish/Chinese/Arabic content. (9) find_earlier_turn_context caps the FTS query limit at 50, defending the index against a misconfigured caller. New tests added: substitution-block-role, marker-fit invariant, recent_n ceiling clamp (kwarg + env var), recent_n boundary equality, consecutive think-only assistant turns, list_notes ceiling.

New services: backend/services/token_counting.py (HuggingFace tokenizer wrapper with char/4 fallback; supports HF_HUB_OFFLINE=1 for deterministic CI), backend/services/compaction_service.py (production retrieval-first compaction policy — recent_n=8, top_k=3, threshold=0.70 per ADR 010 gate verdict; strips <think> blocks; reaches into the markdown vault via find_earlier_turn_context), backend/services/retrieval/sessions.py (session-scoped retrieval entry point that reuses memory_service.list_notes scoped to memory/conversations/, excluding the current session's own saved snapshot). Tunable via JARVIS_COMPACTION_THRESHOLD_PCT (clamped [0.30, 0.95]) and JARVIS_COMPACTION_RECENT_N (clamped at floor of 2). 45 new tests across 5 files; full suite at 1486 passed / 204 skipped. retrieval._note_2026_05_17b: Hybrid retrieval now supports internal path allowlists. `memory_service.list_notes`, `embedding_service.search_similar`, and `search_similar_chunks` can constrain BM25/cosine reads to known note paths, and `retrieval.retrieve(..., path_allowlist=..., deduplicate_folders=False)` lets import-scoped chat rank only manifest-created notes while still using BM25, chunk/note cosine, graph scoring, and reranking. `context_builder` uses this for Folder Source Import suggested questions and falls back to deterministic keyword ranking if hybrid scoring is unavailable.
retrieval._note_2026_05_17: Context assembly now supports `import_batch_id` for Folder Source Import suggested questions. `context_builder.build_context` can read manifest-created note paths, rank those notes with deterministic keyword overlap, wrap them in normal retrieved-note evidence tags, and emit trace entries with `via=import_batch`. retrieval._note_2026_05_05: Embedding-model swap per ADR 018 (v1 English-only). `paraphrase-multilingual-MiniLM-L12-v2` (multilingual, 384-dim, ~252 MB) → `snowflake/snowflake-arctic-embed-l` (Apache-2.0, English-only, 1024-dim, ~1 GB). Highest English MTEB Retrieval avg (55.98 nDCG@10) of any candidate clearing the audit's hard constraints — a ~20-point category jump over the multilingual incumbent (~36 retrieval). Arctic-Embed-L requires a query prefix (`Represent this sentence for searching relevant passages: `); documents are embedded raw. backend/services/embedding_service.py now exposes `embed_query` / `aembed_query` (with prefix) separately from `embed_text` / `aembed_text` / `embed_texts` / `aembed_texts` (document-side, no prefix). Three internal query callers (`search_similar`, `search_similar_chunks`, `find_similar_nodes`) updated. Vector dimension changed 384 → 1024; the SQLite `embeddings` BLOB format is dimension-coupled but v1 has no users so no migration needed. desktop/scripts/fetch-bundled-models.sh updated to pull arctic-embed-l (~1 GB) into `_bundled_models/fastembed/`; PyInstaller spec already wildcards `_bundled_models/fastembed/` so the new model is bundled automatically. ADR 003 §A bundled-ML-weights table updated. Tests: 21 embedding service tests pass; 61 retrieval/graph dependents pass.
retrieval._note_2026_05_05b: Reranker-model swap per ADR 018 — closes the last open commercial-licensing audit blocker on the retrieval pipeline. `jinaai/jina-reranker-v2-base-multilingual` (CC-BY-NC-4.0, downloaded at runtime ~1.1 GB) → `BAAI/bge-reranker-v2-m3` via `onnx-community/bge-reranker-v2-m3-ONNX` (Apache-2.0, INT8 ONNX ~570 MB). The English-first re-research confirmed the v1 pick — v2-m3 still beats every smaller English-only alternative on BEIR (~51.8 vs ~49.5 for `bge-reranker-large`, ~49.6 for `jina-v1-turbo-en`, ~46.9 for `bge-reranker-base`). The onnx-community port is not in fastembed 0.8.0's built-in registry (qdrant/fastembed#494), so backend/services/reranker_service.py registers it via `TextCrossEncoder.add_custom_model(...)` on first use. Drops the previous `Xenova/ms-marco-MiniLM-L-12-v2` runtime-download fallback per the audit's offline-first guidance (bundled .app trusts its bundle; falls through to fusion-only retrieval if the model fails to load). Bundled cache_dir resolution mirrors embedding_service.py — both models share `_bundled_models/fastembed/`. Score distribution shifted from Jina v2's [0,1] sigmoid to v2-m3's unbounded logits (typical −8 to +5); the existing min-max normalisation in services/retrieval/pipeline.py handles the shift inherently, no consumer-side change. desktop/scripts/fetch-bundled-models.sh extended to fetch the reranker alongside the embedder (registers the custom model in the heredoc, then triggers download). Smoke test on the dev venv confirmed top-relevant doc ranks first; 32 retrieval tests pass. ADR 003 §A bundled-ML-weights table updated; retrieval.md Signal 5 description updated.

chat-model-probe._note_2026_05_04: Fixed the 'Re-test recommended · Environment changed since the last self-test' banner sticking forever and ignoring the Re-run button. Root cause: `persist_probe_result` (chat_model_probe.py) built the on-disk payload dict but omitted the `catalog_models` field — every other path (`ProbeResult` dataclass, `_result_as_dict` SSE event, `set_user_override` stub, `needs_rerun` reader, `run_chat_model_probe` reconstruction) handled it correctly, so the gap was hidden in test coverage. Symptom chain: probe writes record without `catalog_models` → next mount, `needs_rerun` reads `persisted.get('catalog_models')` as `None` → falls into the pre-snapshot branch and returns `(True, 'no_prior_probe')` → banner shows with the default reason text 'Environment changed since the last self-test' (not one of the four mapped reasons) → clicking Re-run runs the probe again, persists again without the field, banner reappears. Fix: one-line addition to `persist_probe_result`'s payload dict (`'catalog_models': list(result.catalog_models)`). Existing on-disk records remain pre-snapshot until the user clicks Re-run once after restart, at which point the new shape lands and the banner permanently dismisses. Regression test: `test_persist_round_trip_preserves_catalog_models` round-trips the field and asserts `needs_rerun` returns `(False, 'fresh')` against a matching environment. Total unit tests now 43; endpoint tests unchanged at 12. Earlier `_note_2026_05_01d` introduced the `catalog_models` field but the persistence path itself was missed — this note closes that loop.
chat-model-probe._note_2026_05_01d: Fixed `needs_rerun` false-positive that made the 'A new local model is available — re-run to consider it' banner stick forever. The orchestrator breaks on first passing candidate (line 431), so `candidates_evaluated` is a *subset* of the catalog — comparing `set(catalog) - set(evaluated)` always returned the unevaluated tail as 'added'. Re-running produced the same subset, so the banner reappeared on every page mount; users reported clicking re-run did nothing. Fix: `ProbeResult` gains a `catalog_models: tuple[str, ...]` field, snapshotted from `candidate_list` in `iter_probe_events` and persisted alongside `candidates_evaluated`. `needs_rerun` now compares `current.catalog_models - persisted.catalog_models`. Pre-snapshot records (missing the new field) force a fresh probe via `no_prior_probe` so the new shape gets written rather than re-introducing the bug via fallback. Touchpoints: `chat_model_probe.py` (dataclass + iter_probe_events + _result_as_dict + recommend_chat_model + set_user_override stub + needs_rerun), `routers/local_models.py` (ProbeResult reconstruction in event_stream), `tests/test_chat_model_probe.py` (_persisted helper + 2 regression tests + SSE-keys assertion), `tests/test_local_models.py` (one fixture). Frontend banner code in main.vue and ChatModelProbePanel.vue is unchanged — needs only correct backend signal.
chat-model-probe._note_2026_05_01: Probe panel onboarding-variant restructured: the contradictory verdict-card ("RECOMMENDED · No candidate passed") and the post-run terminal log no longer render alongside the "Jarvis is ready" headline. Live events are now gated on probe.running.value (was: events.length>0, which kept the log visible after completion). The post-run state in the onboarding variant is a single quiet `<details>` line `PROBE · <verdict> · ▸` under the CTA, expanding to a left-aligned monospace evidence list. The verdict-card + override picker remain on the settings variant unchanged. Also fixed inherited `text-align: center` from .local-flow__ready that was staircasing the live-event lines. Pure UI/template; no API, composable, or eval changes.
chat-model-probe._note_2026_04_28: ADR 012 install-time chat-model self-test. Three probes per candidate model: (1) correctness — does the model honor think:false and produce a clean answer to 'say hi in one word', regex-checked against thinking-prose patterns; catches the Qwen3-30B-A3B chain-of-thought leak on Ollama 0.18.0 found by ADR 010 §Issue 4. (2) Hardware-fit — does effective_footprint_bytes(entry, default_ctx) sit under 80% of total RAM; reuses the existing helper that the future memory-pressure auto-downgrade also uses. (3) Speed — runs warm-short and chat-realistic-shallow scenarios from the latency harness once each, checks total_ms ≤ 1500 ms and decode_tps ≥ 8 (calibrated against the 14.6s-broken-vs-1.8s-snappy threshold from the M5 Pro 24 GB findings). Orchestrator iterates user-pickable catalog entries largest-first, hardware-fit prefilter is cheapest so runs first, correctness next (~5-10s per candidate), speed last (~10-20s per candidate, two scenarios); first passing candidate wins, with safe-fallback to None when nothing passes. Persistence to app/config.json under chat_model_probe key via locked_config_update; user_override field lets a user force a specific model unconditionally. 27 unit tests covering thinking-prose pattern panel (8 leaked + 7 clean cases), hardware-fit at threshold edges + KV-architecture differences, orchestrator with stubbed Ollama (largest-first iteration, safe-fallback when nothing passes, hardware-fit prefilter skips before Ollama call), persistence round-trip + non-clobbering of other config keys + override-vs-recommendation precedence. Backend service lands in this chunk; onboarding wiring (frontend) and settings re-run UI are subsequent chunks per ADR 012 migration path.
chat-model-probe._note_2026_04_29: ADR 012 wiring landed end-to-end. Backend additions: iter_probe_events() async generator yielding {started, candidate_start, candidate_evidence, complete} events (recommend_chat_model() now drains it for the blocking path); current_environment(*, ollama_version) capturing (version, platform-with-macos-major, sorted catalog ollama_models); needs_rerun(persisted, current) → (bool, reason) with reasons no_prior_probe / ollama_version_changed / platform_changed / catalog_added_models / fresh — catalog *removals* don't re-trigger; set_user_override() supports setting/clearing on either an existing record or an empty config (stub-record path). Three new HTTP routes in local_models.py: GET /api/local/chat-model-probe (status + needs_rerun + env), POST /api/local/chat-model-probe/run (SSE-stream events; persists on complete; preserves prior user_override across re-runs; refuses 503 when Ollama unreachable to avoid wasted fail_unreachable rounds), POST /api/local/chat-model-probe/override (set/clear, no probe re-run). Tests grew by 30: 18 new unit tests in test_chat_model_probe.py covering needs_rerun across all reason codes (including catalog-shrink-doesn't-trigger), current_environment macOS-major capture, set_user_override on empty/existing/clear, iter_probe_events sequence + complete-payload-key-shape parity with persistence; 12 new endpoint tests in test_local_models.py::TestChatModelProbeEndpoints covering GET fresh/no-prior, run-refused-when-Ollama-down, override get+clear, SSE streaming + persist, user-override preservation across re-runs. latency-benchmark._note_2026_04_28c: Amendment 3 to ADR 011: canonical baseline-0 captured against qwen3:14b on M5 Pro 24 GB / Ollama 0.18.0 / stock knobs (artifact: tests/eval/latency/baselines/apple-m5-20260428T102349Z.json). Per-scenario p50 TTFT / TPS / p95 total: warm-short 151/20.3/310; prefill-4k 163/14.5/732; prefill-16k 194/12.1/869; chat-realistic-shallow 154/14.0/1809; decode-throughput 157/13.5/21643. latency-benchmark._note_2026_04_28b: Amendment 2 to ADR 011 fixed three harness gaps surfaced by the first 14B grid: (1) reference scenarios were duplicated per Ollama model — split scenarios in run_grid so reference runs once per grid; (2) models not pulled in Ollama errored 5× per scenario — added _probe_pulled_models(base_url) hitting GET /api/tags once at run start, absent models record a single skip_reason cell instead; (3) no per-cell sample response text in baseline JSON, forcing curl-based investigation of wrong-shape outputs — aggregate() now captures first non-errored response_text truncated to 500 chars into a new sample_response_text field. Schema additions are additive (skip_reason: Optional[str], sample_response_text: str), schema_version stays at 1. Floor test updated to allow skipped cells. Three new unit tests cover the fixes.
latency-benchmark._note_2026_04_28: ADR 011 latency benchmark harness — sibling to ADR 010's conversation eval. Captures TTFT / decode_tps / total_ms per (model, scenario, seed) cell on the actual ICP hardware (single-machine first: Apple M5 Pro 24 GB). Five v1 scenarios (warm-short, prefill-4k, prefill-16k, decode-throughput, chat-realistic-shallow) + one Anthropic reference shot for the explicit competitor benchmark. Streaming Ollama HTTP /api/chat client captures TTFT from first content delta and decode_tps from done-event eval_count/eval_duration (model-self-reported, excludes HTTP overhead). Bootstrap-CI gate on paired-cell metric diffs (LOWER_IS_BETTER for TTFT/total_ms, HIGHER_IS_BETTER for decode_tps), same statistical floor as conversations/gate.py. CLI run_bench.py supports --scope nightly|pr; PR mode is the floor-gate subset (~5-10 min) for fast checks, nightly is the full grid (~45-90 min on M5 Pro 24 GB) for canonical baseline capture. Stable-key JSON output committed to baselines/, anchored by machine_info (model_label, ram_gb, ollama_version, knob_stack); diffs across baselines surface which knob produced which effect. Opt-in floor test JARVIS_LATENCY_BENCH=1 mirrors test_baseline_floor.py discipline; skips silently until baseline-0 is captured. 28 unit tests covering scenario shape determinism, streaming-timing logic with stubbed httpx, percentile aggregation, machine-info capture, and bootstrap-CI verdict logic across all four cases (improvement/regression/equivalent/insufficient_data). No production-path changes — pure dev infra. Substrate landed in this chunk; baseline-0 capture + knob-loop chunks (flash_attention, kv_cache_q8, prefix_caching verify, speculative decoding) follow per ADR 011 migration path.

ingest-benchmark._note_2026_04_29b: Amendment 5 to ADR 013: knob-6 (background/lazy embedding for section-split ingest) LANDED. User-perceived ingest of 911Report.pdf dropped 25.6s → 1.53s (16.7× faster); background embed catches up in ~24.5s with per-note progress visible via /api/memory/ingest/status. Total compute unchanged (~26s) — knob-6 is a perception change not a compute change. Cumulative since pdfplumber baseline: ~60s → 1.53s = ~40× user-perceived speedup on first-ingest. Implementation in three small changes: (1) memory_service._index_note + index_note_file accept defer_embedding: bool = False; when True, embed_note + embed_note_chunks are skipped. (2) ingest._emit_document_sections passes defer_embedding=True to all index_note_file calls (index + sections), then schedules ingest_jobs.schedule_embed_for_paths BEFORE Smart Connect runs sync on the index. Smart Connect embeds query content on the fly and reads embeddings of OTHER notes — works fine without this document's own embeddings. (3) ingest_jobs.schedule_embed_for_paths spawns daemon thread with start_job/update_stage/finish_job for UI; underlying embed_paths(paths, *, workspace_path, job_id) coroutine is exposed for direct test use. Single-file ingest path (memos, short PDFs) untouched — embedding stays inline (no benefit to deferring sub-second work). JARVIS_DISABLE_EMBEDDINGS=1 short-circuits the scheduler so tests don't leak daemon threads. fast_ingest response now includes embed_job_id alongside section_connect_job_id. Three pivots before landing: (a) tested CoreMLExecutionProvider on the existing FP16 model and confirmed silent fallback to CPU (vectors bit-identical, cosine=1.0, no speedup) — model uses ORT contrib ops (SkipLayerNormalization, Attention, FastGelu) that CoreML EP can't handle; (b) confirmed no int8 variant exists in qdrant repo (only model_optimized.onnx FP16, 224MB); (c) self-quantizing for knob-4 became 'build calibration set + custom-model loader + quality eval + workspace migration story' which is high-risk for compliance product. Pivot to knob-6 paid off — same compute, decoupled from user perception. Three new tests in test_embedding_service.py: defer_embedding=True skip behavior, default behavior unchanged regression, embed_paths() catches up exactly the deferred set. Eventual-consistency cost surfaced: notes not searchable until embed catches up (~24s on 60-section doc), but UI already shows per-note 'indexing N/M' badge and graph view uses wiki-links (written sync) so unaffected. Process death during embed window flagged: notes indexed but unembedded; recovery via reindex_all_chunks exists but isn't automatic — startup scan flagged for follow-on if it becomes a complaint.
ingest-benchmark._note_2026_04_29: Amendment 4 to ADR 013: knob-3 (ONNX Runtime threading) investigated and CLOSED WITHOUT A LANDING. Hypothesis from Amendment 2 was 'free ~2× if currently single-threaded' — wrong on both counts. Current code constructs TextEmbedding without threads= kwarg, so fastembed leaves intra_op_num_threads=0 (ORT auto-pick = physical core count). Two-pass measurement on M5 Pro 24 GB (4 P-cores + 6 E-cores = 10 logical): (1) single 64-text batch micro-bench suggested threads=4 was 1.4% faster than auto (484ms vs 490ms), confirming the 'auto over-subscribes E-cores' hypothesis; (2) production-scale 5,546-chunk pass INVERTED the result — auto/256/none = 40.93s wins, threads=4/256/none = 42.72s loses by 4%, batch_size=1024 loses by 18%. The micro-bench fooled us because at scale work spans ~22 successive ORT calls and auto-pick keeps all 10 cores busy across overlapping batches; single-batch sync penalties amortize away. Only positive signal: parallel=4 (fastembed multiprocessing) at 37.31s = 9% faster, but at cost of +1.6 GB resident memory (4 model copies of 400MB each), almost-certain regression on small docs (per-call worker spawn), and breaks chat-path embedding singleton warm-cache. Recorded as deferred sub-knob to layer with knob-4 (int8) where halved model size makes the parallelism win compound; not its own landing. Net: no code change, no new baseline JSON. The 43s embed_batch floor is real on this (model, ONNX-config) — headroom now lives in model-level changes (knob-4 int8 / knob-5 smaller English-only) or hardware-accelerator backend (knob-7 MLX), not threading. Knob-3 is the first knob in the loop to close without landing; the measurement paid for itself by ruling out a category of 'easy' wins and re-pointing the loop.
ingest-benchmark._note_2026_04_28: ADR 013 ingest-pipeline latency benchmark harness — third benchmarking surface alongside ADR 010 (conversation quality) and ADR 011 (chat-path latency). Times every stage of the document-ingest pipeline (extract → section_detect → chunk → embed_batch → entity_extract) plus end-to-end wall clock against samples/911Report.pdf. Six v1 scenarios: end-to-end + five stage-isolated. Stage-isolated scenarios share prepared upstream inputs across the warm-up + N timed runs so the measurement reflects only the target stage's variance. Reuses paired-bootstrap CI gate machinery from tests.eval.latency.gate (one statistical floor across all three benchmarking surfaces). 34 unit tests covering scenario shape, stage-dispatch correctness, timing capture, units metadata, error propagation, aggregate percentiles, stage ordering, gate semantics, missing-fixture skip behavior. Opt-in floor test JARVIS_INGEST_BENCH=1 mirrors latency floor test discipline. Canonical baseline-0 captured (apple-m5-20260428T124627Z.json on M5 Pro 24 GB / Ollama 0.18.0 / stock pipeline). Headline finding: PDF extract via pdfplumber is 97% of end-to-end on the 585-page 911 Report (19.7s of 20.3s); every other stage combined is under 600 ms. Embed-batch at 64 chunks is 500 ms (~127 chunks/s); production embed_note_chunks loops note-by-note so 5,005 chunks × single-call overhead ≈ 40s of redundant embedding cost on this document — surfaced by the harness, fix is its own chunk. Knob loop sequencing: PyMuPDF replacement first (expected 5–10× extract speedup, biggest win), then parallel page extraction (M5 Pro 12-core), then batched embeddings in production loop, then verify content-hash skip on re-ingest. No production-path changes in this chunk — pure dev infra.
ingest-benchmark._note_2026_04_28b: Amendment 1 to ADR 013: knob-1 (pypdfium2 replaces pdfplumber) landed. PyMuPDF disqualified upfront on AGPL/commercial license (incompatible with closed-source enterprise distribution); pypdfium2 (Apache-2.0/BSD-3, Python bindings to Google's PDFium) chosen as the right speed-AND-license fit. Implementation in backend/services/ingest.py::_extract_pdf_text — single-threaded per call (PDFium not thread-safe), explicit PdfPage/PdfTextPage close per page so memory stays bounded on multi-hundred-page docs. backend/requirements.txt swapped pdfplumber==0.11.9 → pypdfium2==5.7.1. Baseline-1 captured (apple-m5-20260428T131344Z.json, knob_stack=[pypdfium2_extract]): extract 19,686ms → 358ms (55×). Quality verified before swap: pypdfium2 produces 2.6% more chars, 15% fewer arbitrary line breaks, identical page count, identical key content; on multi-column tables pdfplumber was actually creating false-adjacency hybrid sentences which is worse for embeddings. Net: speed win + quality equivalent-or-better, no regression. Distribution-time obligation flagged for release-eng: ship LICENSES/LicenseRef-PdfiumThirdParty.txt in installer THIRD-PARTY-NOTICES.
ingest-benchmark._note_2026_04_28c: Amendment 2 to ADR 013: harness was under-reporting end-to-end. Baseline-0 reported 20.3s and baseline-1 reported 950ms because run_end_to_end capped embedding at the first 64 chunks (embed_batch_size=64) while production's embed_note_chunks embeds ALL chunks of a note in one aembed_texts call. Fix: IngestScenario.embed_batch_size default changed 64→0 (=embed all); the isolated embed_batch(64) scenario keeps its explicit cap for per-batch throughput. Honest baseline-2 captured (apple-m5-20260428T135114Z.json, knob_stack=[pypdfium2_extract,honest_e2e_embed]): end-to-end 43,059ms p50 with breakdown extract 376ms / sections 11ms / chunk 71ms / embed_batch 42,588ms (98.8%) / entity_extract 8ms. Recalibrated knob-1 verdict: pypdfium2 saved ~17s (true baseline-0 was ~60s with all chunks embedded, baseline-2 is 43s) — ~30% end-to-end improvement, NOT 21×; the 55× extract speedup is real but extract was a smaller fraction of total than the cap-bug numbers suggested. embed_batch is now 98.8% of end-to-end and is the bottleneck for knobs 2-7.
ingest-benchmark._note_2026_04_28d: Amendment 3 to ADR 013: knob-2 (chunk-level content-hash skip on re-ingest) landed in backend/services/embedding_service.py::embed_note_chunks. Implementation: snapshot existing (content_hash → embedding_blob) rows before delete, reuse binary blob for chunks whose hash matches, only embed new/changed chunks. Vectors are bit-identical for unchanged content (raw bytes reused, not re-encoded). Five new unit tests in test_embedding_service.py cover identical-content skip, partial-edit isolation, blob bit-identity, full-replacement of obsolete rows, additive new chunks. Production fast_ingest measurements on samples/911Report.pdf (M5 Pro 24 GB): first ingest cold 25.6s; re-ingest of unchanged content 1.4s (18.6× faster, 24.2s saved); re-ingest with one paragraph edited 2.0s (12.9× faster, 23.6s saved). Surfaces the harness/production gap: harness measures compute-only end-to-end with whole-document chunking (5,546 chunks, 43s); production fast_ingest section-splits the PDF first into 60 per-section MD files then chunks each (3,023 chunks, 25.6s) — production is ~40% lighter than the harness suggests because section-split reduces total chunk count. Both numbers are real but track different things; knob iterations should track both. Two follow-ups flagged: (a) update harness end-to-end to mirror section-split path, (b) add re-ingest scenario to harness as a permanent regression-gate metric. Combined knob-1+knob-2 cumulative saving on the accidental-re-upload path: ~58s vs original pdfplumber + DELETE-and-rebuild. First-ingest of 911Report at ~26s is the new floor for this fixture on this hardware until knobs 3-7 land (ONNX threading, quantized model, smaller English-only model, background/lazy embed, MLX).

local-models._note_2026_05_07b: Spurious 'A new local model is available — re-run to consider it' banner on every fresh cold-launch fixed. Root cause: `services/first_run_orchestrator._run_probe` reconstructed the `ProbeResult` dataclass from the chat-model-probe event payload but omitted `catalog_models`, so the field defaulted to an empty tuple and `persist_probe_result` wrote `"catalog_models": []` into `app/config.json`. On every subsequent boot, `chat_model_probe.needs_rerun()` computed `set(current_catalog_models) - set([])` = the full catalog, returned `(True, "catalog_added_models")`, and `pages/main.vue` mapped the reason to the 'A new local model is available' copy — the banner could never dismiss because the snapshot it compared against never landed on disk. Fix: the orchestrator's ProbeResult reconstruction now includes `catalog_models=tuple(payload.get("catalog_models") or ())`, carrying the snapshot the probe ran against through to disk. The blocking `recommend_chat_model` wrapper at `chat_model_probe.py:511` already had this — only the orchestrator's variant was missing it. New regression test in `test_first_run_orchestrator.py::TestPipelineFailures::test_run_probe_persists_catalog_snapshot` mocks `iter_probe_events` and `probe_runtime`, drives `_run_probe`, and asserts the persisted `chat_model_probe.catalog_models` equals the event-payload list — fails before the fix, passes after. Caught during the 4th G4b6 cold-launch test of the day; visible on the wizard's 'Jarvis is ready' screen as the yellow 'Re-test recommended' banner.

desktop-shell-graduation._note_2026_05_06c: Bundled-model cache hygiene: `desktop/scripts/fetch-bundled-models.sh` gained an allowlist-based scrubber that removes orphaned `models--*` dirs from `backend/_bundled_models/fastembed/` before each fetch. Discovered during P2 readiness check for G4b6 — the cache still contained the pre-audit-cleanup multilingual MiniLM (~240 MB, `models--qdrant--paraphrase-multilingual-MiniLM-L12-v2-onnx-Q`) even though `services/embedding_service.py:_MODEL_NAME` and `fetch-bundled-models.sh:MODEL_NAME` had both been swapped to `snowflake/snowflake-arctic-embed-l` weeks earlier. The PyInstaller spec at `desktop/sidecar/jarvis-sidecar.spec:192` bundles the entire `_bundled_models/fastembed/` directory, so without scrubbing, the .app would have shipped 240 MB of orphaned MiniLM weights alongside the real arctic-embed-l + bge-reranker-v2-m3 — contradicting THIRD-PARTY-NOTICES.md (which only declares the new models) and presenting a buyer-audit gotcha ("what model are you actually shipping?"). New `EXPECTED_MODEL_DIRS` array in the script is the single source of truth for which `models--*` dirs are allowed; anything else is removed before the cached-skip check. Same shape as the `_BUNDLED_TOKENIZER_IDS` allowlist pattern in `services/token_counting.py`. Idempotent: safe to re-run, no-op when the cache is already clean. The stale 240 MB MiniLM was deleted manually in this commit; the structural fix prevents recurrence. Logic verified via fixture-based dry-run (orphan dir created in /tmp, scrub correctly removed it while preserving both allowlisted dirs and `CACHEDIR.TAG`).
desktop-shell-graduation._note_2026_05_06b: Build pipeline split: `.dmg` notarize+staple+verify extracted from `desktop/scripts/build-notarized.sh` into a new `desktop/scripts/build-dmg.sh`. Pre-G4b6 prep — the original script ran `.app` notarization (steps 5a) then `.dmg` notarization (5b) under a single `set -e`, so a transient hdiutil/notarytool/stapler flake on the `.dmg` aborted the whole 30-min pipeline even though the `.app` was already fully signed and stapled. The split preserves the `.app` work across `.dmg`-phase failures: `build-notarized.sh` now finishes its `.app` verification, prints the `.app` path, then delegates to `build-dmg.sh` with a friendly retry hint on failure (`bash desktop/scripts/build-dmg.sh`). Credential resolution (keychain profile preferred, APPLE_ID/APPLE_PASSWORD env-var fallback) is duplicated rather than extracted because each script is small and runtime-independent — extraction was the alternative considered and rejected as premature. `build-dmg.sh` accepts an explicit `.dmg` path as `$1` (defaults to tauri's release output) so the operator can re-target a moved/renamed `.dmg` without editing scripts. Both scripts pass `bash -n` syntax check; `build-dmg.sh` mode 0755. No functional change to credential discovery, signing, notary submission, or stapling — only the failure-blast-radius and retry ergonomics. This is a prerequisite for G4b6 cold-launch verification (where each rebuild costs ~25 min and bug-finding requires multiple iterations).
desktop-shell-graduation._note_2026_05_06: Build-time CI assertion added (closes commercial-licensing-audit.md findings #9 + #10). `desktop/scripts/build-sidecar.sh` now scans the produced sidecar binary's archive TOC for the path fragments `samples/`, `911Report.pdf`, `tests/eval/fixtures/`, `reference_workspace/`, and `reference_pdfs.json` after PyInstaller runs. If any are present, the build aborts with a remediation message pointing at the likely cause (a recent edit to `desktop/sidecar/jarvis-sidecar.spec` that added one of these paths to `datas` or extended a `collect_submodules` call past `tests.eval.latency`). Same `strings | grep -F` shape as the existing cloud-SDK-leak assertion (audit finding #2). desktop-shell-graduation._note_2026_05_05: Bundle leakage hardening per ADR 015 amendment. Commercial-licensing audit (docs/research/commercial-licensing-audit.md §2) found that the spec was still hidden-importing `litellm` + `litellm.llms` + `tiktoken_ext` + `tiktoken_ext.openai_public` and `collect_data_files`-ing `litellm` + `tiktoken` + `tiktoken_ext` even though ADR 015 had removed those packages from `requirements.txt`. The dev venv at audit time had `litellm-1.83.7` installed as leftover from the pre-ADR-015 era, so any build executed against that venv would silently re-bundle the cloud SDKs. Three layers of fix: (1) stale entries removed from `desktop/sidecar/jarvis-sidecar.spec` `hidden` and `datas`, with inline comments explaining *why* they're deliberately absent; (2) belt-and-suspenders — `litellm`, `anthropic`, `openai`, `tiktoken`, `tiktoken_ext`, `google.generativeai`, `google.generativelanguage` added to the `Analysis(excludes=...)` list so PyInstaller errors at build time if production code ever imports them (this contradicts ADR 015 chunk 6's 'delete excludes block' instruction — kept on purpose because the ADR's optimistic premise that 'there is nothing to exclude' was empirically wrong); (3) `desktop/scripts/build-sidecar.sh` gains a post-build audit gate that scans the one-file binary's archive TOC for `litellm/`, `anthropic/`, `openai/`, `tiktoken_ext/` path fragments and aborts with a remediation message if any leak is detected — the runtime-enforced version of the ADR §'Audit verification' table's row 2. ADR 015 amended in place with a 2026-05-05 hardening section.
desktop-shell-graduation._note_2026_05_01: Wired persistent file logging in `backend/scripts/run_frozen.py::_setup_logging`. Discovered while validating the per-turn `chat_turn` diagnostic added under chat 2026-05-01d: the Tauri shell drains the sidecar's stdout/stderr only until the READY handshake then drops the receiver (lib.rs:82-110), so anything `logger.info(...)` printed after READY went into a closed channel. Combined with `uvicorn.Config(log_level='warning')` this meant INFO logs from the bundled .app were doubly invisible. Fix: install a `RotatingFileHandler(5 MB × 3)` on the root logger at INFO level, writing to platform-conventional locations — `~/Library/Logs/DeepFilesAI/sidecar.log` on macOS, `%LOCALAPPDATA%\DeepFilesAI\Logs\sidecar.log` on Windows, `${XDG_STATE_HOME:-~/.local/state}/DeepFilesAI/logs/sidecar.log` elsewhere. Idempotent on re-init: existing FileHandlers pointing at the same path are removed first so dev hot-reload doesn't duplicate-emit. uvicorn's own `log_level='warning'` is unchanged — only app loggers (`routers.*`, `services.*`) flow into the new handler at INFO, keeping uvicorn access/error noise out of the file. Operator workflow: `tail -f ~/Library/Logs/DeepFilesAI/sidecar.log | grep chat_turn` while running chat turns in the bundled app.

New service backend/services/first_run_orchestrator.py owns the ADR 005 §B state machine: idle → probing → pulling_primary → (marker write) → pulling_fallback → running_probe → complete (or skipped / failed). desktop-shell-graduation._note_2026_04_30e: G4b4 (memory-pressure monitor + downgrade ladder runtime integration) landed. New service backend/services/memory_pressure_monitor.py exposes synchronous helpers — current_free_ram_bytes() (psutil wrapper, returns 0 on import failure to force floor refusal), check_can_run(entry, ctx_len, *, free_ram_bytes, headroom_fraction=0.80) (pass if effective_footprint_bytes ≤ free × headroom), pick_runnable_model(requested, *, tier, ctx_len_tokens, installed_ollama_tags, ...) which walks downgrade_ladder_for(tier) from the requested model toward the floor and returns MemoryPressureSwap (chosen, did_swap, reason, per-step trail of (model_id, runnable|over_footprint|not_installed)).
